does: This paper identifies and analyzes successor heads, a recurring attention head circuit that increments tokens with natural orderings such as numbers, months, and days across large language models. The authors use mechanistic interpretability techniques to reverse-engineer how these heads work, discovering that they rely on abstract 'mod-10 features' to implement successor operations. They demonstrate that successor heads appear consistently across multiple model architectures and scales, from 31 million to 12 billion parameters, including GPT-2, Pythia, and Llama-2, indicating these circuits represent a fundamental computation that language models learn to perform.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability research program, which has previously identified interpretable circuits in small toy models but struggled to scale insights to frontier models. The paper builds on prior discoveries of modular, reusable components in neural networks by showing that interpretable circuits actually persist and scale to realistic large language models. It connects to the broader literature on how language models represent structured information and learn algorithmic procedures, while responding to the gap between toy model discoveries and actual understanding of large model internals.
enables: By demonstrating that interpretable, mechanistically understandable circuits emerge consistently across model scales and architectures, this work suggests that scaling up mechanistic interpretability from toy models to production systems is feasible. The discovery of successor heads as a reliable, recurring computation opens pathways for analyzing other algorithmic capabilities in large models, potentially leading to better techniques for probing model reasoning, improving model interpretability at scale, and developing more trustworthy AI systems through bottom-up understanding of how neural networks implement discrete operations.
